summary.intent: Optimize the `saveGitOidMapping` call in the indexer to avoid redundant DB reads on every index pass for every checkpoint.
summary.outcome: Implemented a pre-fetch optimization using a Map<checkpointId, gitOid> to skip or upsert mappings only when necessary, with correct handling for rebased branches where the same checkpoint ID may map to a new git OID.

repo: The indexer has two key functions: `indexAllCheckpointsV2` (orchestrator) and `indexCheckpointV2` (per-checkpoint logic), with `saveGitOidMapping` called inside the latter.
The Prisma schema uses `gitOid` as `@id` on the git-OID mapping table, meaning the mapping is keyed by OID, not checkpointId — upsert logic must account for this.
After a git rebase, the same checkpointId can legitimately map to a new gitOid, so presence-only checks are insufficient; the full (checkpointId, gitOid) pair must be compared.

code.finding: saveGitOidMapping was doing a findFirst DB query per checkpoint on every pass; refactored to accept a pre-fetched Map<checkpointId, gitOid> and skip/upsert based on exact pair comparison.
indexAllCheckpointsV2 now does a single findMany upfront to populate the Map, then passes it into indexCheckpointV2 as an optional parameter.
gitOid is the @id field on the OID mapping model, not checkpointId — this is the key schema constraint that drives the upsert strategy.

workflow: Build verification with `npm run build` was used after each refactor step to catch TypeScript compilation errors early.

The optimization evolved mid-session: an initial Set-based approach was replaced by a Map-based approach after the user clarified the rebase use case — design requirements should be fully captured before implementing.

friction: Initial optimization used a Set<checkpointId> which was logically incorrect for the rebase case; required a second redesign pass to use Map<checkpointId, gitOid> instead.
open_items: The in-memory Map is populated once at the start of a pass; if multiple concurrent index passes run, they may read stale data — concurrency safety was not addressed.
After a successful new mapping is created, the Map is updated in-memory for consistency within a pass, but the correctness of this across edge cases (e.g., partial failures) was not verified.
The upsert behavior when gitOid changes (rebase case) should be tested end-to-end to confirm the Prisma schema allows it given gitOid is @id.
